---
title: "Lecture 27: Cookies & Session Tracking"
subtitle: "{{< meta course.number >}} - {{< meta course.title >}}"
author: "{{< meta instructor.name >}}"
date: "March 11, 2026"
code-annotations: below
---

## {#title-slide data-menu-title="Title Slide" background="#053660"}

<div class="custom-title">
[Lecture 27]{.baby-blue-text}
</div>

<div class="custom-subtitle">
[Cookies & Session Tracking]{.white-text}
</div>

<div class="custom-subtitle3">
Week 7 | Unit III: Servlets<br>
{{< meta course.number >}} - {{< meta course.title >}}<br>
{{< meta instructor.name >}}, {{< meta instructor.title >}}
</div>

---

## {data-menu-title="Learning Objectives"}

[Learning Objectives]{.slide-title}

<hr>

::: {.incremental .body-text-m}
- Understand why HTTP alone cannot remember users
- Explain cookies and sessions in simple terms
- Implement basic login tracking in servlet apps
- Compare cookies, URL rewriting, and HttpSession
:::

---

## {data-menu-title="Stateless HTTP"}

[Why Session Tracking is Needed]{.slide-title2}

<hr>

::: {.body-text-m}
HTTP is **stateless**.

That means each request is independent.

Without session tracking:

- server cannot remember who logged in
- cart items disappear between pages
- personalized data cannot continue
:::

---

## {data-menu-title="Cookie Basics"}

[What is a Cookie?]{.slide-title}

<hr>

::: {.body-text-m}
A cookie is a small key-value text data stored in browser.

- Set by server
- Sent back by browser in later requests
- Useful for preferences, user id tokens, language, etc.
:::

```{.java}
Cookie c = new Cookie("username", "rahul");
c.setMaxAge(60 * 60); // 1 hour
response.addCookie(c);
```

---

## {data-menu-title="Read Cookies"}

[How to Read Cookies]{.slide-title2}

<hr>

```{.java}
Cookie[] cookies = request.getCookies();
if (cookies != null) {
  for (Cookie c : cookies) {
    if ("username".equals(c.getName())) {
      String user = c.getValue();
    }
  }
}
```

::: {.body-text-m}
Always check `cookies != null` to avoid null pointer errors.
:::

---

## {data-menu-title="Cookie Limits"}

[Cookie Limitations]{.slide-title}

<hr>

::: {.incremental .body-text-m}
- Limited size (around 4 KB per cookie)
- User can delete/disable cookies
- Not safe for passwords or sensitive data
- Sent with requests, so too many cookies can slow traffic
:::

---

## {data-menu-title="Session Basics"}

[What is HttpSession?]{.slide-title2}

<hr>

::: {.body-text-m}
`HttpSession` stores user data on server side.

You can store attributes per user login session.

Example data:

- user id
- role
- cart items
- temporary messages
:::

```{.java}
HttpSession session = request.getSession();
session.setAttribute("user", "rahul");
```

---

## {data-menu-title="Read Session"}

[Reading Session Values]{.slide-title}

<hr>

```{.java}
HttpSession session = request.getSession(false);
if (session != null) {
  String user = (String) session.getAttribute("user");
}
```

::: {.body-text-m}
Use `getSession(false)` when you only want existing session, not a new one.
:::

---

## {data-menu-title="Login Example"}

[Simple Login Tracking Flow]{.slide-title2}

<hr>

```{.java}
// Login servlet (success case)
HttpSession session = request.getSession();
session.setAttribute("loggedInUser", email);
response.sendRedirect("dashboard.jsp");
```

```{.jsp}
<!-- dashboard.jsp -->
<%
  HttpSession s = request.getSession(false);
  String user = (s == null) ? null : (String) s.getAttribute("loggedInUser");
  if (user == null) {
    response.sendRedirect("login.jsp");
    return;
  }
%>
<h2>Welcome, <%= user %></h2>
```

---

## {data-menu-title="Logout"}

[How Logout Works]{.slide-title}

<hr>

```{.java}
HttpSession session = request.getSession(false);
if (session != null) {
  session.invalidate();
}
response.sendRedirect("login.jsp");
```

::: {.body-text-m}
`invalidate()` clears all session data and marks session invalid.
:::

---

## {data-menu-title="Tracking Methods"}

[Session Tracking Techniques]{.slide-title2}

<hr>

| Method | Where data stored | Good for | Caution |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cookies | Browser | Preferences, simple tokens | User can disable/edit |
| HttpSession | Server | Auth state, per-user data | Memory usage on server |
| URL Rewriting | URL query/path | Fallback when cookies disabled | URL can expose data |

---

## {data-menu-title="Common Mistakes"}

[Common Mistakes to Avoid]{.slide-title}

<hr>

::: {.incremental .body-text-m}
- Storing password in cookie
- Forgetting session null checks
- Not invalidating session on logout
- Sending sensitive data in URL parameters
- Assuming cookies are always enabled
:::

---

## {data-menu-title="Exam Focus"}

[AKTU Exam-Oriented Checklist]{.slide-title2}

<hr>

::: {.body-text-m}
Prepare these short-answer points:

1. Define cookie and session
2. Difference between cookie and HttpSession
3. How to create, read, and delete cookie
4. How to create session and invalidate on logout
5. Why HTTP needs session tracking
:::

---

## {data-menu-title="Summary"}

[Summary]{.slide-title}

<hr>

::: {.body-text-m}
You are now ready to:

- explain session tracking in beginner-friendly language
- write cookie and session code in servlet projects
- build basic login-logout flows
- answer common AKTU theory and practical questions
:::
